title: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema
authors: Mark A. Creager, Robert T. Eberhardt, Joseph Loscalzo

items.subtopic: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema | CHRONIC VENOUS DISEASE
items.title: Prevalence and Impact of Chronic Venous Insufficiency
items.content: Understanding the prevalence of chronic venous insufficiency is crucial for anticipating its impact on patient populations. It affects a significant portion of adults, with higher rates in women compared to men.
keyPoints: Estimated prevalence of varicose veins: ~15% in men and 30% in women.
Chronic venous insufficiency with edema affects ~7.5% of men and 5% of women.
Prevalence increases with age, from 2% among those <50 years to 10% of those 70 years.
Approximately 20% of patients with chronic venous insufficiency develop venous ulcers.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema | CHRONIC VENOUS DISEASE

items.subtopic: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema | CHRONIC VENOUS DISEASE
items.title: Clinical Progression from Chronic Venous Insufficiency to Ulceration
items.content: A significant proportion of patients with chronic venous insufficiency progress to develop venous ulcers, highlighting the importance of early identification and management.
keyPoints: Approximately 20% of patients with chronic venous insufficiency develop venous ulcers.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema | CHRONIC VENOUS DISEASE

items.title: Understanding the Venous System of the Lower Extremities
items.content: The venous system in the lower extremities is divided into superficial and deep veins. Superficial veins, such as the great saphenous vein and small saphenous vein, lie between the skin and deep fascia. The great saphenous vein is notable for being the longest vein in the body, originating on the medial side of the foot and draining into the common femoral vein. Deep veins accompany major arteries and include paired peroneal, anterior tibial, and posterior tibial veins that converge to form the popliteal vein.
keyPoints: Superficial veins are located between the skin and deep fascia.
The great saphenous vein is the longest vein in the body.
Deep veins accompany major arteries and include peroneal, anterior tibial, and posterior tibial veins.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 1)

items.title: Pathophysiology of Varicose Veins
items.content: Varicose veins are dilated superficial veins that measure at least 3 mm in diameter. They can be primary, resulting from defective valves and high intraluminal pressure, or secondary, caused by venous hypertension due to deep-venous insufficiency or obstruction. Factors such as aging, pregnancy, hormonal therapy, obesity, and prolonged standing contribute to primary varicose veins.
keyPoints: Varicose veins are superficial veins measuring at least 3 mm in diameter.
Primary varicose veins result from defective valves and high intraluminal pressure.
Secondary varicose veins are due to venous hypertension from deep-venous insufficiency or obstruction.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 1)

items.title: Chronic Venous Insufficiency and Its Causes
items.content: Chronic venous insufficiency is characterized by incompetent veins leading to venous hypertension and fluid extravasation. It can be primary, due to intrinsic abnormalities in vein walls or valves, or secondary, often following deep-vein thrombosis. Secondary deep-venous insufficiency results from valve incompetence and high pressures distending the vein.
keyPoints: Chronic venous insufficiency involves incompetent veins causing venous hypertension.
Primary insufficiency is due to intrinsic abnormalities in vein walls or valves.
Secondary insufficiency often follows deep-vein thrombosis, leading to valve incompetence.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 1)

items.title: Distinguishing Primary vs. Secondary Varicose Veins
items.content: Bedside maneuvers such as the Brodie-Trendelenburg test help differentiate primary varicose veins from those secondary to deep-venous insufficiency.
keyPoints: Brodie-Trendelenburg test: Filling of varicose veins within 30 seconds after tourniquet removal indicates deep-venous insufficiency.
Primary varicose veins fill promptly after tourniquet is removed without delay, indicating superficial venous insufficiency.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 2)

items.title: Identifying Deep-Vein Obstruction
items.content: The Perthes test assesses the presence of deep-venous obstruction by observing changes in varicose vein filling during walking.
keyPoints: Perthes test: If superficial veins distend further with walking, deep-vein obstruction is likely present.
A patent deep-venous system and competent perforating veins cause the superficial veins below the tourniquet to collapse.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 2)

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Leg Edema
items.content: The duration of leg edema helps distinguish chronic venous insufficiency from other causes such as acute deep-vein thrombosis and lymphedema.
keyPoints: Chronic venous insufficiency is characterized by persistent edema, stasis dermatitis, or skin ulceration near the ankle.
Bilateral leg swelling may indicate congestive heart failure, hypoalbuminemia, myxedema, or drug effects.
Unilateral causes of leg swelling include ruptured muscles, hematomas, popliteal cysts, and cellulitis.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Testing for Chronic Venous Disease: Role of Venous Duplex Ultrasonography
items.content: Venous duplex ultrasonography is the principal diagnostic test for chronic venous disease, utilizing B-mode imaging and spectral Doppler to detect venous obstruction and reflux. It identifies obstructions through absence of flow or failure of vein collapse under compression, while reflux is detected by prolonged reversal of flow during a Valsalva maneuver.
keyPoints: Venous duplex ultrasonography combines B-mode imaging and spectral Doppler.
Obstruction is indicated by the absence of flow or failure to collapse under compression.
Reflux is identified by prolonged reversal of flow direction during a Valsalva maneuver.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 3) | Chronic Venous Disease

items.title: Management and Treatment Strategies for Varicose Veins and Chronic Venous Insufficiency
items.content: Conservative measures such as leg elevation, avoiding prolonged standing, and wearing elastic support hose are first-line treatments for varicose veins. Compression therapy with stockings or bandages is standard care for advanced chronic venous insufficiency, particularly in patients with edema, skin changes, or ulcers.
keyPoints: Elastic compression stockings (20–30 mmHg) are recommended for simple varicose veins.
Graduated compression therapy is the standard of care for CEAP clinical class C3–C6.
Compression pressures of 30–40 mmHg are more effective for healing venous ulcers.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 3) | Chronic Venous Disease

items.title: Advanced Imaging Modalities in Chronic Venous Insufficiency
items.content: Magnetic resonance, computed tomographic, and conventional venography are rarely required but may be used to identify obstructions or stenosis of major veins, particularly when suspecting pathology that might require intervention.
keyPoints: Advanced imaging is reserved for suspected pathologies requiring intervention.
Used to detect obstruction or stenosis in the inferior vena cava and iliofemoral veins.
Indicated for conditions like previous deep-vein thrombosis, occlusion of IVC filters, or May-Thurner syndrome.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 3) | Chronic Venous Disease

items.subtopic: MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)
items.title: Herbal Supplements in Chronic Venous Insufficiency
items.content: Several herbal supplements are used for their venoconstrictive and anti-inflammatory properties to manage symptoms of chronic venous insufficiency. However, evidence is insufficient to recommend their general use.
keyPoints: No FDA-approved drugs for chronic venous insufficiency treatment.
Herbal supplements include horse chestnut seed extract (aescin), ruscus extract, flavonoids like diosmin and hesperidin, and French maritime pine bark extract.

Meta-analyses suggest benefits in pain reduction, leg heaviness, swelling sensation, and venous ulcer healing with micronized purified flavonoid fraction and pentoxifylline combined with compression therapy.
Insufficient evidence for general use of these supplements.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)
items.title: Endovenous Thermal Ablation Efficacy and Risks
items.content: Endovenous thermal ablation, including laser and radiofrequency techniques, is effective for treating varicose veins with high occlusion rates but carries risks of complications.
keyPoints: Used to treat persistent symptoms and complications of venous insufficiency.
Average 1- and 5-year occlusion rates exceed 90% for laser therapy; slightly less for radiofrequency ablation.
Complications include deep vein thrombosis, pain, paresthesias, bruising, hematoma, and hyperpigmentation.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)
items.title: Sclerotherapy for Varicose Veins
items.content: Sclerotherapy involves injecting a sclerosing agent to cause fibrosis and occlusion of veins, with specific occlusion rates and potential complications.
keyPoints: Approved agents include sodium tetradecyl sulfate, polidocanol, sodium morrhuate, and glycerin.
Used for great saphenous vein or its tributaries; 1- and 5-year occlusion rates are 81% and 74%, respectively.
Complications can be deep vein thrombosis, hematomas, nerve damage, infection, and rare anaphylaxis.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)

items.title: Endovascular Interventions for Chronic Venous Insufficiency
items.content: Endovascular interventions, including catheter-based techniques and stent placement, are options for treating advanced chronic venous insufficiency in patients unresponsive to other therapies. These procedures aim to address iliac vein occlusion or stenosis.
keyPoints: Technical success rates exceed 85% in most series.
Long-term patency is achieved in ~75% of these patients.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 2)

items.title: Surgical Approaches to Venous Bypass and Valvular Reconstruction
items.content: Venous bypass procedures, such as iliocaval bypass and femorofemoral crossover venous bypass, are used occasionally for treating iliofemoral vein occlusion. Surgical reconstruction of deep veins involves valvuloplasty or valve transfer to address valvular incompetence.
keyPoints: Long-term patency rates for venous bypass procedures generally exceed 60% and improve symptoms.
Valvuloplasty may result in ulcer healing in the majority of patients, with somewhat better success than vein transfer.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 2)

items.title: Prevalence and Genetic Basis of Primary Lymphedema
items.content: Primary lymphedema, affecting ~1.15 per 100,000 persons under 20 years old, is more common in females and can be inherited through autosomal dominant or recessive patterns.
keyPoints: Prevalence: ~1.15 per 100,000 persons <20 years of age
Females are affected more frequently than males
Genetic mutations associated with primary lymphedema include FLT4 (Milroy's disease), FOXC2 (lymphedema-distichiasis syndrome), and GATA2 (predisposition to acute myeloid leukemia)
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 3)

items.title: Causes of Secondary Lymphedema
items.content: Secondary lymphedema results from damage or obstruction of lymphatic channels, with common causes including cancer treatment and recurrent bacterial infections.
keyPoints: Common cause worldwide: lymphatic filariasis affecting >120 million individuals
Post-cancer treatment: 13% of breast cancer patients develop arm lymphedema after axillary node dissection; 22% after surgery and radiotherapy
Leg lymphedema occurs in ~15% of patients post-ingual lymph node dissection
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 3)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Staging of Lymphedema
items.content: Lymphedema is typically painless but may cause a heavy sensation; it progresses from soft, pitting edema to fibrotic changes. Diagnosis involves recognizing clinical signs like Stemmer's sign.
keyPoints: Early stage: Soft, pitting edema
Stemmer's sign: Inability to tent the skin at the base of toes
Chronic stage: Edema becomes non-pitting with woody texture due to fibrosis
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 3)

items.subtopic: Stage 0 (or la) | Stage I | Stage II | Stage III
items.title: Understanding Stages of Lymphedema for Accurate Diagnosis
items.content: Lymphedema progresses through distinct stages, each with specific clinical features that guide diagnosis and management. Early recognition and differentiation between stages are crucial for appropriate intervention.
keyPoints: Stage 0 (or la): Latent condition with no evident swelling despite impaired lymph transport; may persist for months or years before overt edema.
Stage I: Fluid accumulation is high in protein content, subsides with limb elevation, and pitting may occur. Increase in proliferating cells noted.
Stage II: Limb elevation rarely reduces tissue swelling; pitting is present but may diminish as excess fat and fibrosis develop.
Stage III: Characterized by lymphostatic elephantiasis with possible absence of pitting, acanthosis, fat deposition, fibrosis, and warty overgrowths.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Stage 0 (or la) | Stage I | Stage II | Stage III

items.title: Optimizing Diagnostic Approaches in Lymphedema
items.content: Physical examination is usually sufficient to diagnose lymphedema, but imaging studies can clarify the cause and differentiate between primary and secondary forms.
keyPoints: Ultrasound and CT are useful for detecting obstructing lesions such as neoplasms.
MRI reveals a honeycomb pattern in the epifascial compartment of affected limbs and distinguishes lymphedema from lipedema.

Lymphoscintigraphy involves radioactively labeled technetium colloid injection, showing absent or delayed filling in primary lymphedema and dilated vessels distal to obstruction in secondary lymphedema.
Lymphangiography is rarely indicated due to complexity and risk but shows absent or hypoplastic channels in primary lymphedema and dilation beneath obstructions in secondary forms.
Optical imaging with near-infrared fluorescence dye provides quantitative imaging of superficial lymph flow, useful intraoperatively.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Stage 0 (or la) | Stage I | Stage II | Stage III

items.title: Management of Lymphedema: Prevention and Therapy
items.content: Patients with lower extremity lymphedema require diligent care to prevent complications such as cellulitis and lymphangitis. Emollients, prophylactic antibiotics, and aggressive treatment of fungal infections are recommended. Encouraging physical activity and leg elevation can help reduce edema. Comprehensive decongestive physiotherapy, including manual lymphatic drainage and the use of compressive garments or devices, is beneficial for managing symptoms.
keyPoints: Prevent cellulitis and lymphangitis with meticulous foot and leg care.
Use emollients to prevent skin drying.
Prophylactic antibiotics can be helpful.
Aggressively treat fungal infections.
Encourage physical activity and frequent leg elevation.
Manual lymphatic drainage as part of decongestive physiotherapy.
Apply multilayered compressive bandages post-massage.
Use graduated compression hose or adjustable garments after volume reduction.
Sequential intermittent pneumatic compression devices can improve quality of life.
Diuretics are contraindicated.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Lymphedema

items.title: Complications and Advanced Treatments for Lymphedema
items.content: Uncontrolled lymphedema may lead to recurrent infections, trophic skin changes, and the rare but serious complication of lymphangiosarcoma. Liposuction combined with decongestive physiotherapy can be considered, especially in postmastectomy cases, to manage lymphedema effectively.
keyPoints: Failure to control edema may cause recurrent infections and trophic skin changes.
Lymphangiosarcoma is a rare but lethal complication.
Consider liposuction with decongestive physiotherapy for treatment.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Lymphedema

items.question: What is the estimated prevalence of varicose veins in women in the United States?
items.explanation: The estimated prevalence of varicose veins in women in the U.S. is ~30%.

items.question: Which age group has a 10% prevalence rate for chronic venous insufficiency with edema?
items.explanation: The prevalence of chronic venous insufficiency with edema is 10% among those 70 years of age.

items.subtopic: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema | CHRONIC VENOUS DISEASE
items.question: What percentage of patients with chronic venous insufficiency develop venous ulcers?
items.explanation: Approximately 20% of patients with chronic venous insufficiency develop venous ulcers.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema | CHRONIC VENOUS DISEASE

items.question: Which vein is the longest in the body?
items.explanation: The great saphenous vein is the longest vein in the body.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 1)

items.question: Where does the small saphenous vein drain?
items.explanation: The small saphenous vein drains into the popliteal vein.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 1)

items.question: Which veins are connected by the median cubital vein in the antecubital fossa?
items.explanation: The median cubital vein connects the basilic and cephalic veins.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 1)

items.question: What is the function of perforating veins in the legs?
options: Connect superficial and deep venous systems
Drain blood from the superficial to deep veins
Allow blood flow from deep to superficial veins
items.explanation: Perforating veins normally allow blood to flow from the superficial to deep veins.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic finding in patients with chronic venous insufficiency?
options: Increased leg circumference and absence of edema
Pitting edema confined to the thighs only
Absence of skin changes such as lipodermatosclerosis
items.explanation: Hyperpigmentation, erythema, and eczema are dermatologic findings associated with venous stasis.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 2)

items.question: What does the Brodie-Trendelenburg test assess in patients with varicose veins?
options: The presence of superficial venous insufficiency
Whether varicose veins are secondary to deep-venous insufficiency
The likelihood of chronic arterial disease
items.explanation: The Brodie-Trendelenburg test determines if varicose veins result from deep-venous insufficiency.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical symptom in patients with venous varicosities?
options: Painful skin ulceration near the ankle
items.explanation: A severe headache is not a typical symptom of venous varicosities.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 2)

items.question: What does the Perthes test assess in patients with varicose veins?
options: The presence of superficial venous insufficiency
Deep-venous obstruction and competence of perforating veins
Superficial vein collapse after tourniquet removal
items.explanation: The Perthes test assesses for deep-venous obstruction and the competence of perforating veins.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 2)

items.question: Which diagnostic test is primarily used to evaluate patients with chronic venous disease?
items.explanation: Venous duplex ultrasonography is the principal diagnostic test for chronic venous disease.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 3) | Chronic Venous Disease

items.question: What does a prolonged reversal of venous flow direction during a Valsalva maneuver indicate?
items.explanation: Prolonged reversal of venous flow direction during a Valsalva maneuver indicates venous reflux.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 3) | Chronic Venous Disease

items.question: Which compression pressure is suitable for most patients with simple varicose veins?
items.explanation: Graduated compression stockings with pressures of 20–30 mmHg are suitable for most patients with simple varicose veins.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 3) | Chronic Venous Disease

items.question: What is the purpose of using elastic support hose in managing chronic venous disease?
options: To prevent progression of varicose veins
To improve symptoms by counterbalancing hydrostatic pressure
To replace the need for surgical intervention
items.explanation: Elastic support hose are used to improve symptoms by providing a counterbalance to the hydrostatic pressure in the veins.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 3) | Chronic Venous Disease

items.subtopic: MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following substances has been suggested by meta-analyses to reduce pain, leg heaviness, and/or sensation of swelling in chronic venous insufficiency?
items.explanation: Meta-analyses have suggested that ruscus extract reduces pain, leg heaviness, and/or sensation of swelling in chronic venous insufficiency.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)
items.question: Which FDA-approved sclerosing agent is commonly used for the treatment of varicose veins?
items.explanation: Sodium tetradecyl sulfate is an FDA-approved sclerosing agent used for the treatment of varicose veins.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)
items.question: What is a potential complication of endovenous thermal ablation procedures?
items.explanation: Deep-vein thrombosis of the common femoral vein is a potential complication of endovenous thermal ablation procedures.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)
items.question: Which procedure involves the use of a rotating wire to injure the endothelium and infusion of a liquid sclerosant?
items.explanation: Mechanochemical ablation involves the use of a rotating wire to injure the endothelium and infusion of a liquid sclerosant.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)

items.question: Which procedure is used to treat iliofemoral vein occlusion?
items.explanation: Iliocaval bypass is used to treat iliofemoral vein occlusion.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 2)

items.question: What is the approximate long-term patency rate for venous bypass procedures?
items.explanation: Long-term patency rates for venous bypass procedures generally exceed 60%.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 2)

items.question: Which procedure involves tightening the valve by commissural apposition?
items.explanation: Valvuloplasty involves tightening the valve by commissural apposition.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 2)

items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with the lymphedema-distichiasis syndrome?
items.explanation: The FOXC2 gene mutation causes the lymphedema-distichiasis syndrome.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 3)

items.question: What is a common acquired cause of secondary lymphedema in tropical countries?
items.explanation: Podoconiosis is a common acquired cause of secondary lymphedema in tropical countries.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 3)

items.question: Which syndrome involves lymphedema praecox and double row of eyelashes?
items.explanation: Lymphedema-distichiasis syndrome involves lymphedema praecox and a double row of eyelashes.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 3)

items.question: Which gene is involved in the development of lymphatic valves and predisposes to acute myeloid leukemia?
items.explanation: The GATA2 gene is involved in the development of lymphatic valves and predisposes to acute myeloid leukemia.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 3)

items.subtopic: Stage 0 (or la) | Stage I | Stage II | Stage III
items.question: Which stage of lymphedema is characterized by early accumulation of fluid that subsides with limb elevation?
items.explanation: Stage I involves early fluid accumulation that subsides with limb elevation.

items.subtopic: Stage 0 (or la) | Stage I | Stage II | Stage III
items.question: In which stage of lymphedema does pitting become manifest and limb elevation rarely reduces tissue swelling?
items.explanation: In Stage II, pitting is manifest and limb elevation rarely reduces tissue swelling.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Stage 0 (or la) | Stage I | Stage II | Stage III

items.subtopic: Stage 0 (or la) | Stage I | Stage II | Stage III

items.explanation: Stage III involves lymphostatic elephantiasis where pitting can be absent.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Stage 0 (or la) | Stage I | Stage II | Stage III

items.question: Which of the following is contraindicated for patients with lymphedema?
items.explanation: Diuretics are contraindicated in lymphedema as they may cause depletion of intravascular volume and metabolic abnormalities.

items.question: What is the purpose of applying multilayered, compressive bandages after massage sessions for lymphedema patients?
items.explanation: Multilayered, compressive bandages are applied after each massage session to reduce recurrent edema.

items.question: Which of the following is a highly lethal complication associated with uncontrolled limb edema in lymphedema?
items.explanation: Failure to control limb edema may lead to a highly lethal complication of lymphangiosarcoma.

items.subtopic: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema | CHRONIC VENOUS DISEASE
items.statement: The prevalence of varicose veins is higher in men than in women.
items.explanation: Varicose vein prevalence is higher in women (~30%) compared to men (~15%).
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema | CHRONIC VENOUS DISEASE

items.subtopic: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema | CHRONIC VENOUS DISEASE
items.statement: Chronic venous insufficiency with edema affects a larger percentage of men than women.
items.explanation: Chronic venous insufficiency with edema affects ~7.5% of men and 5% of women.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema | CHRONIC VENOUS DISEASE

items.statement: The great saphenous vein drains into the popliteal vein.
items.explanation: The great saphenous vein drains into the common femoral vein, not the popliteal vein.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 1)

items.statement: Bicuspid valves are present throughout the venous system to direct blood flow centrally.
items.explanation: Bicuspid valves in veins help direct blood flow towards the heart.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 1)

items.statement: Lipodermatosclerosis typically occurs in the lower part of the leg just above the ankle.
items.explanation: Lipodermatosclerosis is characterized by induration, hemosiderin deposition, and inflammation occurring near the ankle.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 2)

items.statement: A venous ulcer typically has a smooth border and lacks granulation tissue.
items.explanation: Venous ulcers are often shallow with an irregular border and contain granulation tissue.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 2)

items.statement: Compression garments can prevent the progression of varicose veins.
items.explanation: Compression garments improve symptoms but do not prevent the progression of varicose veins.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 3) | Chronic Venous Disease

items.statement: Magnetic resonance and computed tomographic venography are routinely required for diagnosing chronic venous insufficiency.
items.explanation: These modalities are rarely required unless there is suspicion of pathology that might warrant intervention.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 3) | Chronic Venous Disease

items.subtopic: MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)
items.statement: Sclerotherapy is most commonly used to treat the great saphenous vein.
items.explanation: Sclerotherapy is most commonly used to treat the affected tributaries of the great saphenous vein or incompetent perforating veins.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)
items.statement: Endovenous ablation techniques are generally favored over ligation and stripping for the treatment of varicose veins.
items.explanation: Endovenous ablation techniques are preferred due to lower complication rates compared to ligation and stripping.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)

items.statement: Technical success rates for catheter-based interventions exceed 85%.
items.explanation: Technical success rates exceed 85% in most series for catheter-based interventions.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 2)

items.statement: Lymphedema is primarily caused by venous insufficiency.
items.explanation: Lymphedema is caused by impaired transport of lymph, not venous insufficiency.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 2)

items.statement: Primary lymphedema can be caused by agenesis, hypoplasia, hyperplasia, or obstruction of the lymphatic vessels.
items.explanation: The passage states that primary lymphedema may be caused by these conditions.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 3)

items.statement: Lymphatic filariasis is the most common etiology of secondary lymphedema worldwide.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that lymphatic filariasis affects over 120 million people and causes lymphedema in 14 million.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 3)

items.subtopic: Stage 0 (or la) | Stage I | Stage II | Stage III
items.statement: Lymphoscintigraphy is rarely indicated in the diagnosis of lymphedema.
items.explanation: Lymphoscintigraphy is rarely indicated but can confirm diagnosis or differentiate primary from secondary lymphedema.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Stage 0 (or la) | Stage I | Stage II | Stage III

items.subtopic: Stage 0 (or la) | Stage I | Stage II | Stage III
items.statement: MRI cannot distinguish between lymphedema and lipedema.
items.explanation: MRI is useful to distinguish lymphedema from lipedema.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Stage 0 (or la) | Stage I | Stage II | Stage III

items.statement: Physical activity is discouraged in patients with lymphedema.
items.explanation: Exercise may minimize symptoms and maintain functionality, so physical activity is encouraged.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Lymphedema

items.statement: Psychosocial support can assist patients with anxiety or depression related to lymphedema.
items.explanation: Psychosocial support is indicated to help patients cope with anxiety or depression related to body image and self-esteem.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Lymphedema

items.subtopic: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema | CHRONIC VENOUS DISEASE
items.assertion: The prevalence of chronic venous insufficiency with edema increases with age.
items.reason: Prevalence is 2% among those <50 years of age and 10% for those 70 years of age.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema | CHRONIC VENOUS DISEASE

items.assertion: Primary varicose veins result from defective structure and function of the saphenous vein valves.
items.reason: Intrinsic weakness of the vein wall contributes to primary varicose veins.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 1)

items.assertion: Secondary deep-venous insufficiency can result from May-Thurner syndrome.
items.reason: May-Thurner syndrome involves compression of the right common iliac artery on the left iliac vein.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 1)

items.assertion: Chronic venous insufficiency can lead to phlebolymphedema.
items.reason: Venous insufficiency impairs lymphatic function, leading to combined venous and lymphatic issues.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 2)

items.assertion: The Brodie-Trendelenburg test is used to diagnose arterial insufficiency.
items.reason: This test involves assessing venous filling in response to tourniquet application and leg elevation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the test assesses deep-venous insufficiency, not arterial insufficiency.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 2)

items.assertion: Graduated compression stockings with pressures of 30–40 mmHg are more effective for healing venous ulcers.
items.reason: Higher pressure stockings provide better support and improve venous return compared to lesser grades.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why higher pressures are more effective.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 3) | Chronic Venous Disease

items.assertion: Antibiotics should be used routinely in patients with venous ulcers.
items.reason: Venous ulcers often become infected, necessitating antibiotic treatment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; antibiotics are not indicated unless the ulcer is specifically infected.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · VENOUS ANATOMY (part 3) | Chronic Venous Disease

items.subtopic: MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)
items.assertion: Endovenous thermal ablation procedures have high occlusion rates.
items.reason: The heat injuries the endothelium and media, promoting thrombosis and fibrosis resulting in venous occlusion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why endovenous thermal ablation procedures have high occlusion rates.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)
items.assertion: Surgical therapy for varicose veins is performed under local anesthesia.
items.reason: The procedure involves ligation and stripping of the great and small saphenous veins.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because surgical therapy is usually performed under general anesthesia, not local.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · MEDICAL THERAPIES | INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 1)

items.assertion: Lymphedema of the leg affects approximately 15% of patients with cancer after inguinal lymph node dissection.
items.reason: Surgical excision or irradiation of axillary and inguinal lymph nodes is a common cause of secondary lymphedema in developed countries.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 3)

items.assertion: Chronic venous insufficiency is a cause of lymphedema termed phlebolymphedema.
items.reason: Phlebolymphedema results from the obstruction of lymphatic vessels by tumors.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as it incorrectly attributes phlebolymphedema to tumor obstruction.
items.reference: Chronic Venous Disease and Lymphedema · INTERVENTIONAL AND SURGICAL THERAPIES (part 3)
